questions.question: In the ViT-MLP-LLM VLM pattern, which component is most commonly trained while the others are kept frozen during alignment, and why?
options: The projector (the 2-4 layer MLP between ViT and LLM) — it maps vision tokens into the LLM's embedding space and carries most of the cross-modal alignment signal while being small enough to train cheaply
The vision encoder — it must adapt its features to the LLM's vocabulary
The LLM — because language generation requires updating all weights
All three components are always trained together from the start
questions.explanation: The standard VLM recipe trains the projector first with ViT and LLM frozen (alignment stage), then optionally unfreezes everything for pre-training and instruction tuning. The projector is small (tens to hundreds of millions of parameters) and takes the brunt of the modality bridge. Fine-tuning in production often trains only the projector plus LoRA on attention layers — cheap and effective for domain adaptation.

questions.question: How does stage 1 (alignment) differ from stage 3 (instruction tuning) in the three-stage VLM training pipeline?
options: Stage 1 and 3 are identical; the stage numbers are just checkpointing conventions
Stage 1 uses LoRA; stage 3 uses full fine-tuning on a larger dataset

Stage 1 freezes the ViT and LLM and trains only the projector on image-caption pairs to bridge the modality gap; stage 3 fine-tunes on curated (image, question, answer) triples to teach conversational behaviour and task formats
Stage 1 uses synthetic data; stage 3 uses only human-annotated data
questions.explanation: Stage 1 (alignment) is cheap: only the projector moves, learning to convert vision tokens into the LLM's embedding space from raw image-caption pairs. Stage 3 (instruction tuning) is where the model learns to answer questions, follow instruction formats, and reason about images — the data is carefully curated QA rather than noisy web captions. Most domain-specific fine-tunes target stage 3 with LoRA, since the projector is already aligned after stage 1.

questions.question: In `MinimalVLM._merge(text_embeds, vision_embeds, input_ids)`, vision embeddings replace positions where `input_ids == self.image_token_id`. Why use image placeholder tokens in the text stream rather than always prepending vision tokens?
options: Special image marker tokens reduce the total sequence length, speeding up attention

Inserting vision tokens at image-token marker positions allows flexible interleaving — e.g. 'Compare <image1> with <image2>' — enabling multi-image and spatially grounded prompts; the same pattern is used by LLaVA, Qwen-VL, and InternVL
The LLM tokenizer rejects vision embeddings unless they occupy a dedicated special token slot in the vocabulary
It speeds up the KV-cache because vision tokens are cached separately
questions.explanation: Always prepending vision tokens forces a fixed position for image content. Placeholder tokens in the text stream let the caller write 'Describe the difference between <image1> and <image2>: ...' with image tokens appearing exactly where the model should attend to them relative to the surrounding text. This is the token-interleaving pattern every production VLM (LLaVA, Qwen-VL, InternVL) uses for multi-image and spatially grounded reasoning.

questions.question: The doc states current VLMs score 50-60% on spatial reasoning benchmarks (above-below, left-right, counting). What does the lesson recommend for production tasks that require precise spatial reasoning?
options: Use a larger VLM (70B+) — scale alone closes the spatial reasoning gap
Use chain-of-thought prompting to help the VLM reason about positions step by step
Always fine-tune the vision encoder, not just the projector

Use a specialised keypoint/pose estimator, depth model, or detection model with box geometry post-processing — tools purpose-built for spatial reasoning are more reliable than a generic VLM on these tasks
questions.explanation: VLMs are trained primarily on image-caption and QA data that does not strongly supervise spatial relationships, so 50-60% on spatial benchmarks is close to chance on some tasks. The lesson explicitly recommends routing spatial-reasoning production tasks to dedicated geometric tools: pose estimators, depth networks, or detectors with geometry post-processing. Validating heavily on the target spatial task is essential before trusting a generic VLM.

questions.question: A production VLM shows high token-generation confidence but the text describes objects not present in the image. Which metric captures this failure, and how is it computed?
options: Perplexity — low perplexity means the model is hallucinating
Accuracy on MMMU — a drop in benchmark score flags hallucination

Cross-Modal Error Rate (CMER) — the fraction of outputs where text confidence is above a threshold but image-text cosine similarity (via a CLIP-family checker) is below a threshold; high CMER signals confident-but-ungrounded generation
Token generation latency — slower generation correlates with hallucination
questions.explanation: CMER is a production alignment KPI introduced by Skywork.ai. It catches the classic VLM hallucination pattern: the model is confident about its text output (high mean per-token probability) but the text is not grounded in the image (low CLIP-based image-text cosine similarity). Monitor CMER per endpoint and prompt type; treating rising CMER as a routing signal to human review cut hallucination rate ~35% in their deployment.

questions.question: Why do modern VLMs use CLIP-family or SigLIP vision encoders rather than a supervised ImageNet ResNet as the backbone?
options: SigLIP and CLIP encoders are faster at inference than ResNets of equivalent depth
SigLIP encoders have more parameters than ResNets, giving richer features
ResNets cannot produce patch tokens compatible with transformer projectors

CLIP/SigLIP encoders are trained jointly with text, so their output space is already approximately aligned with language; the projector needs far less work and the VLM reasons better about visual-textual concepts out of the box
questions.explanation: Vision-language pretraining shapes the encoder's representation to live in a text-compatible space. A supervised ImageNet ResNet was trained without any text signal, so bridging its activations to an LLM requires much larger projectors and far more paired data. CLIP/SigLIP/DINOv3 encoders produce features an LLM can consume with a few linear layers. This is why almost every SOTA VLM — LLaVA, Qwen3-VL, InternVL3.5 — uses a CLIP-family or SigLIP encoder.
